Off Calendar Aug-18-2017 Continued To Oct-18-17 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Aug-31-2017 Sustained
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
E. Michael Linscheid, Esq. (415-782-6002) for Plaintiff/ opposing party Tri State Crab Producers Association.
Dean Von Kallenbach, Esq. (707-427-3998) of Williams Kastner & GibbsPLLC for Plaintiff/ Opposing party Tri State Crab Producers Association through Court Call.
Marc Melnick, Deputy Attorney General (510-879-0750) for Defendant/Moving party.

RULING - ARGUED. The Court announces the basis for the tentative ruling. Plaintiff argues. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT/RESPONDENTS CALIFORNIA DEPARTMENT OF FISH AND WILDLIFE AND DEMURRER TO PETITIONER'S 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT

Respondents California Department of Fish and Wildlife and California Fish and Game Commission's demurrer to all six causes of action in petitioner Tri-State Crab Producers Association's amended complaint is sustained without leave to amend as to all six causes of action. Whether called failure to exhaust administrative remedies, unripe or not necessary or proper as yet (CCP 1061), all six causes of action alleged by Tri-State are premature and must await a concrete factual dispute. While no doubt filed in good faith and for the laudable purpose of avoiding assertedly unnecessary permit revocation proceedings, Tri-State's complaint seeks advisory opinions in the absence of a specific concrete dispute. It is possible that such a dispute may never arise. There is no applicable exception to the requirement for exhaustion of administrative remedies. Tri-State's reliance on Baxter Healthcare Corp. v. Denton (2004) 120 Cal.App.4th 333, 362 is unpersuasive in light of the facts alleged in the complaint. Baxter only applies where an administrative agency's decision is a foregone conclusion, which is not the case here with the Commission. Tri-State has not alleged, nor could it credibly allege, that the Commission will necessarily accept the Department's interpretation of Section 8279.1 if and when the issue arises. The fourth cause of action regarding an excessive penalty and forfeiture, while not technically subject to exhaustion, is not amenable to resolution without a factual record. (See Bajakajian v. United States (1998) 524 U.S. 321, 336 n. 10.) The court exercises its discretion per CCP 1061 to refuse to hear any of the declaratory relief claims. The injunctive relief claim fails because it is not based on any substantive law claim and is also premature. (as fully reported in the reporter's transcripts)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Harold Kahn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Gordon Aiavao, CSR#11216, gaiavao@sftc.org, Reported. =(302/HK)
